narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic records, test scores, and background to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 (below the typical 3.21 average), LSAT score of 38 (slightly above the 36.70 average), Year 1 and Year 3 school deciles both at 2 (well below averages of 5.71 and 5.53), middle family income bracket, and full-time student status—and predicted a 66.5% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This high predicted failure risk led directly to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar. Among all the factors considered, your LSAT score had the strongest positive influence, pushing the prediction toward expecting you to pass despite other concerns. However, your Year 3 decile ranking was the next most influential factor, strongly pulling the prediction toward failure because it reflects weaker performance relative to peers at that stage. Similarly, your Year 1 decile played a key role in the same direction, signaling early academic struggles that the model sees as a red flag for bar success. Compared to typical students who pass, your lower deciles stand out as bigger drags than your solid LSAT helps, even though full-time status aligns with the vast majority (over 92%) of successful applicants. Your middle-income background is common and neutral here. This combination means the model views your trajectory as riskier than average, despite strengths like your LSAT. To move forward, focus on improving your GPA through targeted coursework or retakes, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it further, and gather strong letters of recommendation highlighting your resilience as an Asian male navigating competitive academics. Reapplying after addressing these gaps could shift the prediction in your favor—many students turn things around with deliberate steps. Keep pushing; your LSAT shows real potential.
